risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator ... randomisation is performed to assign patients, in a 1:1 allocation ratio ... with stratification by sex, age ... and assumed duration of illness ... via the minimisation method." Comment: The allocation sequence was generated using a computerized random number generator, which is a truly random method. The use of minimisation/stratification is acceptable here because the random generation method is explicitly described.

Quote: "To avoid biased allocation, randomisation is undertaken by central allocation using a computerized random number generator at the Internet Data and Information Center for Medical Research (INDICE), provided by the University Hospital Medical Information Network (UMIN) in Japan. After written informed consent has been obtained, randomisation is performed to assign patients..." Comment: Central allocation is explicitly described, which is a mechanism that prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the upcoming assignment before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." Comment: This was an open-label intervention with no blinding of participants or personnel. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the main outcomes, especially patient-reported satisfaction and attitudes toward medication, and could also affect staff behavior and care delivery, so performance bias is likely high.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the study, neither the patient nor staff can be blinded." and "The primary outcome is the patient’s satisfaction at discharge as assessed by the Japanese version of the Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ-8J) ... a self-report scale"; secondary outcomes include "the Japanese version of the Drug Attitude Inventory-10 (DAI-10)" (self-report) and "the ward doctor undertakes routinely" the BPRS. Comment: The key assessors here are the patients for the self-report outcomes, and they were explicitly not blinded; patient satisfaction and medication attitude are subjective and therefore vulnerable to detection bias. Although symptom severity is doctor-rated, the trial states staff could not be blinded, so the outcome assessment for subjective measures is at high risk of bias.

Quote: "The data will be analysed on an intention-to-treat principle. Multiple imputation methods [28] will be used for imputing any missing information regarding outcome measures." Comment: The protocol explicitly plans ITT analysis with multiple imputation for missing outcome data, which is an appropriate method for handling attrition and suggests missing data were anticipated and addressed; no evidence is provided of imbalanced or outcome-related dropout that would bias the result.
